deductions: 1_pedagogy/1.3: Little explicit meta-learning/process feedback about why the tutor is using questions/ownership, beyond procedural prompting; minimal discussion of learning strategy or how to develop philosophical methodology. | 2_dialogue_quality/2.1: A multi-turn spiral around role/authorship (student resisting commitments; tutor insisting), slowing substantive progress. | 2_dialogue_quality/2.2: Could have tied responses more explicitly to the full assignment requirement to cover Fuel and Blood earlier; although the tutor did request them, the anchoring to 'must address these cases' could have been stronger/earlier given the drift. | 2_dialogue_quality/2.3: Dialogue does not reach application to Fuel/Blood or a full case-by-case treatment of lecture cases due to missing setups; progression toward completing the whole prompt is incomplete. | 3_communication_quality/3.1: Some turns are longer than needed for an interactive tutoring flow (multi-part directives and extended framing), though generally clear and structured. | 3_communication_quality/3.2: Tone becomes rigid/abrasive during the role/authorship dispute ('Stop.' and admonitions), which could harm rapport.

1_pedagogy/1.1: Provided extensive near-final prose in a 5-paragraph scaffold that could be submitted with minimal student changes, partially blurring tutoring vs doing the assignment. | 1_pedagogy/1.3: Little explicit meta-learning/process feedback beyond stating boundaries; limited explanation of why the question-based method supports learning/ownership. | 3_communication_quality/3.1: One major turn was longer than needed for interactivity, presenting a full multi-paragraph scaffold rather than smaller bite-sized steps.

1_pedagogy/1.1: Several questions became procedural/role-enforcement (YES/NO restatement) rather than Socratic prompts that move the table-building task forward. | 1_pedagogy/1.2: The tutor did not effectively build on the student's strong partial answers (clear scope rationale and proposed sources) to progress to the next actionable step; instead the conversation stalled on role/commitment mechanics. | 1_pedagogy/1.3: Little to no explicit meta-learning/process coaching about how to choose consistent geographies/years or how to document sources beyond insisting the student must decide and bring back IDs; minimal explanation of the tutoring approach. | 2_dialogue_quality/2.1: Major redundancy/spiraling: repeated demands for YES/NO commitment and role correction across multiple turns without synthesizing and moving to alternative offline steps sooner. | 2_dialogue_quality/2.3: Progression toward assignment completion was weak: the conversation did not reach actual table fields/values/extraction guidance because it stayed on commitment and inability to browse; only at the end did the tutor pivot to a table skeleton. | 3_communication_quality/3.1: Some turns were more repetitive/longer than needed given the stalemate (multiple imperative instructions to restate commitment). | 3_communication_quality/3.2: Tone became rigid/combative ('Stop', 'dodging responsibility') and did not adapt to the student's constraint about lacking web access, which likely harmed collaboration.

1_pedagogy/1.3: Little explicit meta-learning/process coaching beyond insisting on student ownership; minimal explanation of learning strategy or how to proceed methodologically until very late. | 2_dialogue_quality/2.1: Major repetition/spiraling: multiple consecutive turns repeat the same 'paste the template' instruction without synthesis or alternate strategy, causing an unproductive loop. | 2_dialogue_quality/2.3: Limited progression toward assignment completion for most of the conversation; tutor stayed stuck on obtaining inputs rather than moving the work forward with a hypothetical example or general sourcing guidance once the student's role confusion persisted. | 3_communication_quality/3.1: Several responses could have been more concise/less repetitive (multiple near-identical template messages).

1_pedagogy/1.1: Tutor prompted the student to draft “3 actual table rows,” which partially blurs tutoring vs producing deliverable content (even though the student wrote them and the tutor later re-established boundaries). | 1_pedagogy/1.3: Little explicit meta-learning/process rationale (e.g., why using questions/ownership) beyond task guidance; mostly procedural prompting without broader methodology coaching. | 3_communication_quality/3.1: A few tutor turns are somewhat long and multi-part where shorter, more bite-sized prompts might have sufficed, though still clear and well-structured overall.

1_pedagogy/1.3: Limited explicit meta-learning/methodology explanation beyond enforcing boundaries; tutor rarely explains the learning strategy (why questions/evidence-first) except as a requirement. | 2_dialogue_quality/2.1: Major redundancy: multiple turns repeat the same insistence that the student must pick a city and provide a link/excerpt, with slow synthesis until late. | 2_dialogue_quality/2.3: Progression toward the deliverable is delayed; several turns are spent on role conflict and logistics before moving to actor identification steps. | 3_communication_quality/3.1: Some turns are longer and more directive than necessary, with repeated instructions that could be consolidated into a shorter checklist earlier. | 3_communication_quality/3.2: Tone becomes rigid/confrontational at points (e.g., 'Stop trying to flip roles'), which may reduce supportiveness even though boundaries are appropriate.

1_pedagogy/1.3: Tutor did not provide meta-learning/methodology feedback beyond asserting roles; no explanation of why questions/inputs are needed or learning strategy, just repeated boundary statements. | 2_dialogue_quality/2.1: Major repetition/spiraling: multiple turns repeat the same request (choose a city/plan) without new synthesis or alternative pathway, leading to stalemate. | 2_dialogue_quality/2.3: Dialogue fails to show progression toward assignment completion; no actors table/discussion development occurred due to deadlock. | 3_communication_quality/3.1: Several turns could have been shorter and more varied; repeated boilerplate prompts contributed to lack of clarity in next actionable step beyond 'pick a city.' | 3_communication_quality/3.2: Tone becomes rigid/confrontational at points (e.g., 'Stop trying…', 'we’re done here'), which can harm collaborative tutoring even if boundary-setting is justified.

1_pedagogy/1.3: Tutor set boundaries and used guided steps but rarely explained the underlying learning rationale (ownership/why questions) beyond 'I can’t write it for you.' | 2_dialogue_quality/2.1: Some repetition/spiraling around getting the student to produce prose (multiple turns insisting on 'write your own sentences') before new progress occurred. | 3_communication_quality/3.2: Tone became somewhat rigid/irritated when correcting the student ('detour and not acceptable'), which could be more supportive while still enforcing boundaries.

1_pedagogy/1.3: Little explicit meta-learning about why the tutor is using questions/ownership beyond a brief boundary statement; limited process feedback beyond content-tightening. | 2_dialogue_quality/2.2: Could have re-tied more explicitly to the full prompt’s required Fuel/Blood applications earlier with a concrete plan for proceeding without them (e.g., locating OCW materials), though the tutor did repeatedly request the texts.
